draft.introduction: The Triṃśikā (“Thirty Verses”) by Vasubandhu is the foundational text of Yogācāra (“Mind-Only”) Buddhism. It argues that what appears as external reality is the transformation of consciousness.

translation.text: Whatever is imagined as self and dharmas—these transformations of consciousness are of three kinds: the maturation of consciousness, the thinking consciousness, and the perception of sense-objects. The first is the “store consciousness” (ālaya-vijñāna), which holds all seeds.
translation.original_text: ātmadharmopacarao hi vividho yaḥ pravṛttate | vijñānaparinamaḥ sa ca trividhaḥ

translation.text: When consciousness no longer apprehends any object, it stands in the nature of consciousness-only. When there is nothing to grasp, there is no grasping. This is the supramundane, non-conceptual knowledge—the revolution of the basis, the elimination of the two kinds of obscuration.

translator_notes.note: The ālaya-vijñāna (store consciousness) is Yogācāra’s most controversial innovation. It functions as a repository of karmic seeds that ripen into experience, solving the problem of karmic continuity without positing a permanent self. It is neither self nor not-self—a stream that maintains identity without essence.
